event_blocks.summary: The AMERICAN THEATRE WING presents WORKING IN THE THEATRE COSTUMES, opening with an upbeat electronic theme music and white text. The scene transitions to a cluttered DESIGN STUDIO, showcasing COSTUME SKETCHES, ART SUPPLIES, a cup of colored pencils, and a BRASS DESK LAMP. William Ivey Long, a costume designer, is then shown seated at a drafting table, meticulously refining a costume sketch of a male figure using pastels and his fingers, accompanied by the distinct sounds of pencil scratching and smudging. He explains his background in set design at Yale Drama School and his transition to costume design due to a lack of jobs in scenery, noting that his first big musical, 'Nine,' led to a Tony Award that changed his life. Visually, his workspace is filled with historical photographs, sketches, color swatches, and tools, illuminated by the brass desk lamp. He describes his creative process as a 'blitzkrieg gathering of images' to combat the fear of not coming up with ideas, demonstrated by his pinning up new sketches and adjusting existing ones amidst period attire designs and historical mugshots, reinforcing his archival research and iterative design process.

event_blocks.summary: In a design studio filled with historical photographs, William Ivey Long, clad in a dark suit and striped tie, emphasized the critical importance of high stakes for aspiring individuals, stating, 'for kids starting out, it has to be life or death.' A percussive transition led to a bright, modern workspace where Emilio Sosa, a costume and fashion designer in a gray flat cap and denim shirt, was surrounded by a mannequin draped in fabric, framed awards, and fashion sketches. A computer screen displayed the 'PANTONE FASHION COLOR REPORT SPRING 2014,' before Emilio was shown at a white industrial sewing machine, guiding patterned fabric amidst spools of thread and a red dish of yellow-headed pins. The sewing machine's whirring and stitching became prominent as Emilio, speaking calmly with a slight accent, revealed his open approach to projects, stating he 'never say no to a project' and always finds a way to 'make it happen.' He reflected on the relationship-driven nature of theater, acknowledging he learned this 'the hard way,' having had 'a chip on my shoulder the size of Manhattan,' and described his 70s New York City upbringing as 'the best of times and the worst of times' during the city's bankruptcy. Later, Emilio discussed his South Bronx upbringing, finding beauty amidst urban decay and the influence of Hip-Hop, graffiti, and vibrant colors on his aesthetic, noting his upbringing is 'always a part of me.' He was then seen examining garments, including a light blue top with a black thorny pattern, and sorting through colorful, embroidered patches with text like 'RAD' and 'MUSICAL,' reinforcing his passion for text and vibrant colors, with a framed 'RAD ANT BABY' artwork visible. The segment concluded with an upbeat electronic music track as Emilio engaged with his creative process. Further narration revealed Emilio's recollection of being asked to audition for 'Project Runway,' needing to 'prove to them that I was a fashion designer' as costume designers were then considered 'a gimmick.' The sounds of rustling paper and a white chalk pencil marking dark, textured fabric were heard as he adjusted a pattern, pinning it with yellow-headed pins from a red dish, amidst a workspace cluttered with tools, fabric swatches, and a mannequin. Emilio's narration continued, reflecting, 'I learned that I have very little patience for when things don't go my way,' with soft, natural lighting highlighting the precision of his work.

event_blocks.summary: In a bright, white-walled design studio, WILLIAM IVEY LONG, in a dark suit, enters and is warmly greeted by EMILIO SOSA, wearing a gray flat cap and denim shirt. Their animated conversation, filled with expressive gestures, immediately focuses on the studio's light, with WILLIAM emphasizing its necessity for his creative process and EMILIO agreeing it aids in color perception. The studio environment is rich with creative elements, including a clothing rack, a mannequin draped in a beige garment, and framed artwork like 'RAD ANT BABY'. EMILIO recounts a pivotal moment from his youth at fashion school, inspired by a famous lace jumpsuit worn by Anita Morris, which he describes as a unique "fashion piece" for theater that significantly altered his perception of costume design. Later, WILLIAM explains a design choice involving a ruffle at the ankle and calf to maintain a leotard's curve, likening it to "flamenco energy" for "va-va-va-voom," a detail that fascinates EMILIO. They discuss the power of theater to transcend generations and boundaries, with EMILIO highlighting how costumes help "people are in a dark sharing a story." The conversation shifts to practical design challenges, such as teaching men to wear pants at their natural waist, with EMILIO humorously describing his methods of using suspenders and stitching to keep them in place. They also touch upon men's fashion, with WILLIAM expressing a longing for historical styles like those of Louis XIV, and EMILIO noting that men, with their more limited wardrobes, rely on suits to "say everything." WILLIAM shares how seeing a "hero's" work on PBS in the late 60s/early 70s inspired him to pursue design as a career, a sentiment echoed by EMILIO's question about making a living from it. They delve into the complexities of designing in black and white, with WILLIAM revealing a trick for softening tones and mentioning 'Willa' as someone who executed this beautifully. Throughout their discussion, the studio is depicted as a hub of creativity, filled with art supplies, a sewing machine, spools of thread, and various garments, all illuminated by even, natural lighting.

event_blocks.summary: In a cluttered yet creative DESIGN STUDIO, WILLIAM IVEY LONG, dressed in a dark suit and striped tie, engages in an intimate conversation with EMILIO SOSA, who wears a gray flat cap and denim shirt. WILLIAM IVEY LONG explains how designers historically "always painted skin tones into the leotards," making it difficult to discern where the color originated, a concept EMILIO SOSA enthusiastically affirms as "the key." WILLIAM IVEY LONG further elaborates on a designer's intent for subjects to appear "basically naked." Their animated discussion, punctuated by expressive hand gestures, includes plans to "go see Willa," whom EMILIO SOSA praises as "amazing though" and "cutting edge." The conversation culminates in a heartfelt embrace, with EMILIO SOSA declaring, "You're my hero," and WILLIAM IVEY LONG reciprocating, "Well, you're my hero," thanking him for "getting today." Transitioning to a professional presentation, WILLIAM IVEY LONG stands alone in the bright, expansive DESIGN STUDIO, surrounded by walls adorned with HISTORICAL PHOTOGRAPHS, FASHION SKETCHES, and COSTUME SKETCHES. He confidently describes his signature outfit, worn "for decades: white shirt, red tie, navy blazer, khaki pants, black shoes," humorously recalling a "we period during the 70s" when he wore blue jeans. He shares a core design philosophy: "The worst thing a costume designer can wear are interesting clothes. Nothing destroys confidence..." The studio, with its large tables covered in sketches, pinned references, and design notes, emphasizes a space of creative collaboration and historical inspiration. Later, WILLIAM IVEY LONG surveys a wall covered in COSTUME SKETCHES and HISTORICAL PHOTOGRAPHS, emphasizing the importance of dedicating "all of your energy and design essence for the work." He recounts the longevity of shows like "Kander and Ebb's Chicago running now in its 18th year" and the challenges of unavailable fabrics, calling it "a wonderful problem to have" when costumes deteriorate. He then turns to interact with a woman in a white blouse. Close-ups reveal HAT FORMS labeled with character names like 'MARTHA CRATCHIT' and 'CARRIE KENEALLY,' highlighting the detailed process. In a dimly lit area with fabric racks, WILLIAM IVEY LONG discusses his passion for collecting fabrics, describing how he accumulates "bins of fabric of 'shoulda, woulda, coulda beens'" from "fabric stores." He explains his work is about "completing a process" and honoring "the memory of that production," jokingly referencing "Hoarders." Pointing to a collage of COSTUME SKETCHES, he identifies it as his "concept board for Cinderella," explaining he maintains "one board that is just for that show" to capture its "essence." He examines a translucent, leaf-patterned garment draped over a MANNEQUIN, explaining his challenge to create "see-through" dresses for Cinderella's "aspirational" ball scene, revealing he "made this little doll" which "worked," despite others "laughing, very indulgently" at "William and his dolls." The studio, cluttered with ART SUPPLIES and a BRASS DESK LAMP, underscores the creative chaos. He refers to a dense arrangement of black-and-white historical photographs of men as "all the gangsters" he's "lined up," discussing a "backstage musical" where a gangster offers money for a show if his girlfriend gets a big role, noting it's an "old story that still happens today." He explains his effort to make his take on gangsters "different" due to current "overexposure." Examining costume sketches laid out on a table and referencing a book titled 'DECO Landmarks,' he notes that "all this color is this, is based on this Art Deco door," and mentions turning images upside down to avoid "reading it." He emphasizes maintaining a "very strict color scheme" for "all these gangsters on stage," wanting them "all to be tied together," interjecting with "Right" as WILLIAM IVEY LONG concludes that "there aren't enough colors in this." The studio environment includes a MANNEQUIN draped in a BEIGE GARMENT, a RED DISH OF PINS, and a BRASS DESK LAMP, reflecting focused collaboration.

WILLIAM IVEY LONG speaks expressively with a calm, articulate voice, reflecting on the importance of being "aware of the body language in a fitting" and the "aha moment" when clients see themselves transformed. Close-ups reveal his hands threading beads with a fine needle, underscoring the intricate craftsmanship and quiet concentration that defines his artistry.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative synthesizes the provided captions into a cohesive, high-density summary. It begins with the intricate hand-sewing of a beaded garment on a mannequin, detailing the process of inventing pleating and creating a ruffled collar, which is described as a "first costume." The scene shifts to a dimly lit design studio, showcasing a man engrossed in his work amidst books, fabric, and tools, with a mannequin nearby. Upbeat, melancholic piano music accompanies a pan through eclectic rooms filled with vintage furniture, plants, a black swan sculpture, and a wooden chest. The focus then shifts to Willa Kim, a costume designer, in a sunlit room at a round table, surrounded by books, a glass jar of eggs, and a colorful lamp. Rustling paper sounds accompany her as she reviews historical photographs and design materials, commenting on news headlines. She later recounts designing costumes for Julie Andrews, explaining how the actress chose her preferred items from a selection. The narrative follows Willa Kim walking through a hallway adorned with framed art and decorations, her footsteps echoing on wooden floors with a green runner. She meets a man in a dark suit and striped tie, and they discuss framed artwork, specifically one depicting an emperor's revival through song. The man reveals this artwork inspired him to become a designer. Their animated conversation continues, with Willa Kim emphasizing that costume design is an art form, not a trade, and revealing she never formally studied design, having always aspired to be an artist. She recounts her early career, starting with "full page ads" for a makeup company, achieving "mad success" and earning $45 a week, before being offered $75 a week by Paramount. Despite initial unhappiness at Paramount, feeling like she was "hanging around sound stages doing nothing" in a "big factory," she was asked by Karinska to be her assistant after creating color samples. This led to meeting "wonderful artists" through Karinska, including Ralph Duvois, who brought her to Paramount for his movie "Lady in the Dark." She reflects on the "extraordinary education" gained from meeting Karinska and Raoul Pène du Bois, whom she identifies in historical photographs alongside Karinska and "Raoul's boyfriend," noting their significance in costumes and sets. The conversation touches upon Nureyev and Balanchine, with Willa Kim clarifying Balanchine's connection to the others. The environment is consistently described as a "cozy, vibrant space" with "rich textures and colors," featuring elements like a patterned floor rug, a black swan sculpture on a wooden chest, potted plants, beaded ornaments, and a colorful lamp, all contributing to an atmosphere of "warmth, creativity, and personal connection."

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of hands meticulously hand-sewing a shimmering, heavily beaded garment on a mannequin, emphasizing the intricate craftsmanship involved in costume design. A male voice describes the process, recalling how he "ran out of thread" and "pulled it," leading him to "invented pleating" and create a "ruffled collar" for his dog, which he proudly calls "my first costume." The scene transitions to a wider view of a dimly lit design studio, where this man is seated at a small table, focused on his work, surrounded by books, fabric, and tools, with a mannequin draped in a beaded dress nearby. Upbeat, slightly melancholic piano music begins to play as the camera pans through a series of rooms, revealing a cluttered, eclectic space filled with vintage furniture, plants, and decorative objects, including a striking black swan sculpture and a large wooden chest. The scene then shifts to Willa Kim, a costume designer, sitting at a round table in a sunlit room. She is dressed in a cream-colored jacket and a pearl necklace, deeply engrossed in her work, surrounded by books, a glass jar of eggs, and a colorful lamp. The sound of paper rustling accompanies her as she flips through a stack of photographs and design materials. Willa Kim's clear, articulate voice is heard, first stating, "I love the news," then reading headlines aloud: "JP Morgan's legal hurdles expected to multiply. Wow. That's all they need," and "Speedy trains transform China," to which she comments, "Well, I would think so." The atmosphere is one of creative immersion, blending historical references with personal artistic expression, underscored by the ongoing piano music and Willa Kim's engaged commentary.

The video opens with a close-up of WILLA KIM, a woman with short dark hair, wearing a cream-colored jacket and a pearl necklace, seated at a round white table in a richly decorated, eclectic room. As she carefully flips through a large photo album or book filled with black-and-white photographs and text, her voice is heard remarking on the content, saying, "Raises questions. Army of questions. I can't believe this." The table is cluttered with books, papers, a glass jar of eggs, and a colorful lamp with a yellow and red shade casting a warm glow, surrounded by large green potted plants, a tall black swan sculpture, and various decorative objects. She continues, "I should try to clean this thing out. It's just awful," accompanied by the distinct rustling of pages. Pausing her browsing, she picks up a book titled 'Julie Andrews,' holding it up and reading intently, then shares, "I designed her costumes for her in one of the shows that she was... I guess the last one she did." She recounts an anecdote about her design process, explaining, "What's so funny is that I ran around and bought a lot of stuff, you know. And she picked what she liked." The camera pulls back to reveal the full cozy, vibrant space with a patterned blue and white floor rug. She concludes her story with a light laugh, "And when it was all over, she asked me what happened to the rest of them. She wanted to keep them." After a moment, WILLA KIM closes the book, gathers her materials, and stands up, walking away from the table. The camera follows her through a dimly lit hallway adorned with framed art and hanging decorations, her footsteps audible as she moves down a narrow corridor with wooden floors and a green runner, eventually disappearing into the distance, shifting the atmosphere from intimate to slightly mysterious.

The scene opens in a dimly lit, narrow hallway, rich with eclectic framed artwork, hanging decorations, and a green patterned runner on wooden floors, creating a warm, intimate, and cozy ambiance under soft, golden overhead lights. Footsteps echo as WILLA KIM, a woman with short dark hair, a cream-colored jacket, and a pearl necklace, walks toward the camera alongside a man in a dark suit and striped tie. The man greets her warmly, exclaiming "Willa!" to which she responds, "I knew you." He playfully comments, "Oh, you're dressed," and when she mentions she was about to change, he insists, "Oh, don't change. Never change." As they pause, looking upward at the decorations, Willa gestures, and their animated conversation continues. The man notes, "I haven't been here in a while," before asking about a specific piece of FRAMED ARTWORK: "Isn't this from the Rossignol?" Willa confirms, "Uh-huh," and prompts him, "You remember it." He affirms, "I remember it. I remember it. I, it's crazy. I..." Willa then vividly recounts the artwork's story: "It's one of the emperor who is on his deathbed and about to die, and Rossignol sings and then he wakes up and so he doesn't die." The man adds that while the original is in color, he saw a black and white PBS broadcast of it, which "made me want to become a designer," revealing his past in art history and aspiration to write books. Willa acknowledges his passion, saying, "I know, I know you are." The camera closely follows their warm interaction amidst the decorative elements, including plants and a tall black swan sculpture, as they share their appreciation for the artistic environment.

The video opens in a dimly lit, artistically decorated hallway, where WILLA KIM, a woman with short dark hair, a cream jacket, pearl necklace, and glasses, walks alongside a MAN IN DARK SUIT AND STRIPED TIE. Both are engaged in animated conversation, their voices clear and lively. The man, speaking in a deeper tone, praises Willa Kim's design work, stating, "you had designed the scenery and costumes and props," and humorously adds, "you're the exact reason I wanted to do this for a living, this crazy thing we do." Willa Kim, with a higher-pitched voice, laughs heartily, responding, "It's my fault," and then proudly declares, "that's the best thing I've done." As they move through the eclectic space, adorned with hanging beaded ornaments, framed artwork, and plants, Willa Kim gestures expressively towards the decorations. The man laughs again, and Willa Kim continues, calling their shared profession "an addition to our trade, our craft." The man interjects, "It's a trade," but Willa Kim playfully corrects him, "No, no, it's an art. It's an art form," revealing her lifelong aspiration: "I always wanted to be an artist." They pass through a beaded curtain into a cozy room with a large colorful lamp and a round table covered with books. Willa Kim sits, continuing her conversation, emphasizing, "Don't tell me I'm a tradesperson." She then shares a surprising detail, "I never studied design," to which the man responds with a surprised "No?" Willa Kim clarifies, "No, I didn't know it existed. I was an artist," before the audio cuts off as she mentions having "a job with..." The soft, warm lighting throughout emphasizes the rich textures and colors, creating an atmosphere of warmth, creativity, and personal connection.

The segment opens with a close-up of WILLA KIM, a woman with short dark hair, wearing a cream-colored jacket, a pearl necklace, and red lipstick, seated in a warmly lit, eclectic room. She speaks animatedly, gesturing expressively, recounting her early career, stating she was doing "full page ads" for a "makeup company in LA" within two weeks, calling it "a mad success" and that she was "making all this money," which she clarifies was "$45 a week." The camera alternates between her and a MAN IN DARK SUIT AND STRIPED TIE, who listens intently. Willa continues, explaining that "Paramount called" and "offered me $75 a week." The Man in Dark Suit responds with an audible "Oh my goodness!" showing his surprise. Willa then recounts telling a man at the makeup company that she was "doing what I want to do," but he advised her to take the Paramount offer because "not many designers get that offer," so she went. Her expression shifts to thoughtful as she reflects, "what am I doing here? I'm, you know, this isn't what I want to do," describing her unhappiness "hanging around sound stages doing nothing" and feeling it was "all a big factory." The camera pulls back to reveal a wider view of the room, showing them seated at a ROUND TABLE cluttered with books and a colorful lamp, while Willa continues her story, describing how "this stately woman walked by me." The cozy, artistic atmosphere is enhanced by hanging beaded ornaments, framed artwork, a large green vase, and in the background, a hallway with a BLACK SWAN SCULPTURE on a WOODEN CHEST and potted PLANTS.

The scene opens on WILLA KIM, a woman with short dark hair, wearing a cream-colored jacket, a pearl necklace, and red lipstick, seated at a white round table in a richly decorated, eclectic room. She speaks animatedly, her clear, articulate voice recounting her early days at Paramount, stating, "I didn't know what I was doing there. I was just hanging around Paramount." She continues, explaining how "she asked me to do some color samples for her for these sketches," revealing the person to be "Karinska," who then requested, "I want that girl to be my assistant." WILLA KIM gestures expressively as she speaks, at one point resting her chin on her hand, then pointing to a large open book filled with black-and-white historical photographs, perhaps illustrating her narrative. She emphasizes the significance of Karinska, noting, "It wasn't just meeting designers, you know, Hollywood designers... you'd think of them as trades people or something." She then highlights the positive impact, stating, "through her, I met all these wonderful artists that she knew." Across from her, a MAN IN DARK SUIT AND STRIPED TIE listens intently, occasionally responding with gestures and smiles. He interjects with a calm, deeper voice, asking, "Who were they, some of the artists that you you met with, Madame Karinska there?" WILLA KIM promptly replies, naming "Ralph Duvois," who "brought her to Paramount" to do his movie, "Lady in the Dark." The man acknowledges with "Oh, I see." The table is cluttered with books, papers, a glass jar of eggs, and a colorful lamp with a yellow and red shade that casts warm light over the space. The camera zooms in on WILLA KIM's expressive face before pulling back to reveal the cozy, artistic room, complete with a patterned blue rug, a tall black swan sculpture on a wooden chest, hanging beaded ornaments, and large potted plants, all contributing to the intimate and intellectual atmosphere of their deep conversation.

The scene opens in a richly decorated, eclectic room where WILLA KIM, in a cream-colored jacket and long pearl necklace, sits at a ROUND TABLE opposite a MAN IN DARK SUIT AND STRIPED TIE. The table is cluttered with books, papers, and a colorful lamp, casting a warm glow. WILLA KIM animatedly narrates an anecdote, gesturing expressively as she recounts visiting MGM and being told she was sitting next to Marlene Dietrich, expressing her surprise and humor at not recognizing her. She continues, mentioning Nureyev and Balanchine, as the camera zooms in on her hands holding a small, round object. The MAN IN DARK SUIT AND STRIPED TIE listens intently, occasionally nodding, and interjects with a question, asking, "And which is Raoul? Raoul Balanchine?" WILLA KIM corrects him, pointing to historical photographs in a large open book, identifying "Raoul back here, right next to Karinska," and adding, "That's Raoul's boyfriend." The MAN responds with an audible "Oh." WILLA KIM then reflects on her experiences, stating, "I tell you, it was an an extraordinary education. If I wanted to be a designer, I couldn't have picked the two most important people in in costumes and sets than Karinska and Raoul Pène du Bois." The MAN then asks, "And where does Balanchine fit into this?" as WILLA KIM begins to explain his connection. The room's artistic aesthetic, with a BLACK SWAN SCULPTURE, large green PLANTS, and a patterned FLOOR RUG, provides a nostalgic backdrop to their professional and intimate conversation about historical costume design.

event_blocks.summary: Willa Kim, seated at a round table cluttered with books and a colorful lamp, discusses an actress and a 'stage door Johnny' with a man in a dark suit and striped tie, who inquires about choreographing a movie. Kim then walks down a hallway adorned with framed artwork, pointing out a piece and mentioning 'Isabelle' and 'Barishnikov' as beautiful dancers. Transitioning to a studio space with a drafting table covered in art supplies, the man asks for direction on placing an item, confirming 'Out in the hall, okay.' Kim dips a brush into water, remarking on the enjoyment of looking at paints, and begins to paint on a large sheet of paper. The man guides her, expressing surprise at her technique and identifying a painted image as 'a ballet' and 'the bottom of a tutu.' Later, Kim, wearing glasses and a cream jacket with a pearl necklace, paints watercolors at the drafting table, explaining her deep involvement in dance from the initial idea to movement expression, comparing it to plays but noting differences in audience awareness. The studio, filled with natural light, books, and costume sketches, fosters concentration and collaboration, punctuated by clicking sounds. She recounts an anecdote about a play fitting with two superior women and a friend asking about a "wonderful article" by Jonathan Miller in the New York Times. Kim reflects on her career, emphasizing vulnerability as a young designer and the importance of not 'short-changing' the director, stating such experiences 'harden themselves' for future work. The studio walls display a wall collage of historical photographs, decorative objects, and an Academy of Television Arts & Sciences award from 1981 for 'The Tempest,' alongside more photographs and memorabilia, including a large image of a woman in an elaborate costume.

The scene transitions to a bright, well-lit studio space where WILLA KIM, dressed in a cream-colored jacket and a long pearl necklace, is deeply engaged in painting with watercolors at a large white drafting table. The drafting table is cluttered with various art supplies, and subtle scratching and rustling sounds accompany Willa's precise work. She continues to paint, occasionally pausing to adjust her grip or consult the man, while her voice elaborates on the creative process, stating, "You're involved in dance from the very beginning... you see a choreographer talks to you about an idea, then he or she is working on expressing that idea in movement." The camera then shifts to a bright, cluttered studio, filled with pinned costume sketches, books, and art supplies, where WILLA KIM, a woman with short dark hair, wearing a cream-colored jacket and a long pearl necklace, is seated at a drafting table. She is deeply immersed in painting with watercolors, her hands steady as she applies color to a large sheet of paper depicting a figure in flowing garments. As she paints, she speaks in a clear, conversational voice, explaining, "You're involved with the idea of what the dance is about." A man in a dark suit and striped tie stands attentively beside her, listening and occasionally offering guidance. The video opens in a cluttered, creative studio where WILLA KIM, a woman with short dark hair, a cream-colored jacket, and a long pearl necklace, is seated, her expression thoughtful and animated as she speaks. She reflects on her career, stating, "I didn't have to defend myself at all," a point to which the MAN IN DARK SUIT AND STRIPED TIE, seated beside her at a DRAFTING TABLE, interjects, "in print." event_blocks.summary: Willa Kim, a woman with short dark hair, a cream-colored jacket, and a long pearl necklace, sits in a cluttered studio space, speaking reflectively and gesturing expressively about learning from experiences. raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of WILLA KIM, a woman with short dark hair, wearing a cream-colored jacket and a long pearl necklace, seated in a cluttered yet vibrant studio space. She speaks with a calm, reflective voice, gesturing expressively as she explains, "It toughens you, but also you learn from these things." The scene then transitions to a wide shot of a large, well-lit design studio, where a man in a dark suit and striped tie enters from a hallway and approaches a massive drafting table covered in papers and sketches. During this transition, lively jazz music with clarinet, drums, and bass plays in the background. The studio walls are densely adorned with COSTUME SKETCHES and HISTORICAL PHOTOGRAPHS, creating a rich visual backdrop. The man begins to work at the table, leaning over to examine and adjust the sketches, using a BRASS DESK LAMP for focused light, surrounded by ART SUPPLIES like watercolor palettes and paintbrushes. The camera then cuts to a close-up of the man, who speaks directly to the camera with a clear, articulate voice and animated hand gestures, his face illuminated by the studio’s overhead lights. He elaborates on life's stages, stating, "I always say, three things. When you're starting out, you're a puppy dog... you're so excited, you're jumping up, knocking people over." He continues, "Then, the second moment in life... is knowledge. You learn, you understand, you have knowledge, you make discerning choices, and you're a professional." Concluding, he adds, "The third and final stage is wisdom. And wisdom has the effect of you sit in your comfortable chair," as the segment ends, emphasizing the creative intensity and professional focus of costume design.

MAN IN DARK SUIT AND STRIPED TIE.description: A man in a dark suit and striped tie, wearing glasses, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, his face illuminated by studio lighting, standing in front of a wall covered with historical photographs and costume sketches, who states, 'You have your single malt Scotch right at your hand, and you go, you know, maybe we just don't do that,' and adds, 'I just hope wisdom doesn't take over.'

raw_segments: The minute opens with a close-up of a man in a dark suit and striped tie, wearing glasses, speaking directly to the camera in a studio environment. Behind him, a wall is densely covered with black-and-white historical photographs and costume sketches, creating a textured, layered backdrop. The man gestures expressively as he speaks, his face illuminated by soft studio lighting, stating, "You have your single malt Scotch right at your hand, and you go, you know, maybe we just don't do that." He continues, "I just hope wisdom doesn't take over." As he finishes speaking, lively, upbeat instrumental music, reminiscent of traditional folk or polka, begins to play. The scene then transitions to a dimly lit storage or backstage area, where the same man is is seen walking through a narrow, cluttered corridor lined with shelves filled with books, boxes, and costume materials. He moves purposefully toward a brighter area at the end of the hallway, his silhouette briefly visible against the light, all while the cheerful music continues. The video then cuts to a series of black screens displaying white text credits, listing roles such as Producer/Director, Executive Director, Cinematographer, Editor, Sound, Production Assistant, Original Music, and Graphics. The music persists as acknowledgments appear for individuals and organizations like Willa Kim, Brian Mear, Donald Sanders, William Ivey Long Studio, Emilio Sosa Studio, and The Dorothy Strelsin Foundation. The final frames, still accompanied by the instrumental music, thank public funding bodies, including The New York State Council on the Arts and The New York City Department of Cultural Affairs, before the screen fades to black and the music fades out, leaving silence.
